data.title: Hershey CEO: Building Long-Term Resilience to Higher Cocoa Costs — Market Talk
data.description:
1554 ET - Hershey CEO Michele Buck says at the Consumer Analyst Group of New York conference that the company is taking steps to build long-term resilience to fluctuations in cocoa prices. These actions include "diversifying our supply chain in terms of where we source cocoa, how we think about product formulation, and investing in innovative solutions, both on the ground in cocoa production, as well as using cocoa science in other ways to boost the business," she says. ​Record-breaking cocoa prices are pressuring earnings among candy companies, though Buck reiterates the company is confident in its ability to hit its 2025 targets as cocoa costs normalize. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1549 ET - Treasury yields rise, snapping a two-day losing streak, as investors expect sluggish housing data and look for hints on the interest rates outlook. January housing starts likely contract 8.6%, in a Wall Street Journal survey, after expanding 15.8% in December. Today, February's NAHB housing market index fell more than expected, to 42 from 47. Fed minutes are due tomorrow afternoon. Future markets are pricing at least one cut this year, CME data show. The 10-year rises 0.067 percentage point, to 4.542%. The two-year rises 0.039 p.p. to 4.297%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1543 ET - The Make America Healthy Again movement could add to Mondelez International's costs as the company is already struggling with high cocoa prices. The effort, spearheaded by Health and Human Services head Robert F. Kennedy Jr., could require Mondelez to change the ingredients it uses in products like Oreo and Cadbury, Chief Executive Dirk Van de Put said at the 2025 Consumer Analyst Group of New York conference. Van de Put expects new rules would be based on European standards, where Mondelez already distributes a range of food products, so reformulating North American snacks would be "relatively straightforward." There would still be some additional expense, he said, as the sweets maker is already juggling high ingredient prices and cost-wary consumers. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1523 ET - Inflation-weary shoppers are favoring off-price retailers, such as Burlington, Marshalls and T.J. Maxx. In 4Q, the off-price apparel category claimed a majority of combined off-price and traditional apparel visits for the first time since at least 2019, Pacer.ai data shows. "This demonstrates the segment's strong holiday performance and continued resilience in the face of economic headwinds for both consumers and retailers," the location analytics company writes. Several off-price retailers, including Burlington and Ross Dress for Less, expanded their retail footprints last year, which likely contributed to their increased year-over-year visits. And most plan to continue expanding this year, setting them up for continued foot-traffic growth, Placer.ai says. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1506 ET - Baird analysts expect Walmart to report solid 4Q results before the market opens Thursday, likely ahead of Wall Street forecasts, they say in a research note. The Street has high expectations--with views that top the company's guidance--but the retailer has been over-delivering as of late, the analysts say. Plus, top-line indicators, such as Placer.ai data that shows stable U.S. traffic, are supportive, they add. Walmart is well positioned to issue a strong outlook as well: "The business continues to gain share across channels/categories, and improving trends in general merchandise are expected to continue (likely narrowing the gap with grocery and health/wellness)," the analysts write. Baird maintains its outperform rating and raises its price target 15% to $115. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1458 ET - Gold futures rise 1.7% to a record $2,931.60 a troy ounce, closing in on $3,000/oz. "Both the fundamental and technical near-term outlooks continue to strengthen the bullish sentiment," Peter Cardillo of Spartan Capital says. Demand for gold from global central banks has also been a factor supporting gold, as has demand from gold ETFs. SPDR Gold shares are up 1.8% in afternoon trading. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1324 ET - WillScot Holdings' initiated cash dividend has two sides to consider. On the one hand, the dividend of 7 cents a share should be well-received and help open up its investor base, says Jefferies' Philip Ng in a research note, though he would prefer excess cash be diverted to M&A. While the dividend may soothe things over after choppy results and stock performance over the last two years, Ng says it may also raise questions that inorganic opportunities are more narrow after it and fellow portable-building company McGrath Rentcorp in September called off a roughly $3 billion merger. Shares are up 1% to $38.40. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1300 ET - Fast-food owner Yum Brands said it plans to shift some of its corporate operations out of Louisville, Ky., the longtime home of its KFC brand. The company says that 100 U.S. corporate KFC workers will be asked to report to Plano, Texas, home to Yum's Pizza Hut division. The chain is asking 90 other U.S. workers now serving in remote roles to begin reporting to the campus where their work occurs. Yum will maintain two U.S. headquarters in Plano and Irvine, Calif, while it will have a smaller footprint in Louisville. (heather.haddon@wsj.com; @heatherhaddon)

1157 ET - General Mills is investing in pet food as it is seeing consumers spend more on their furry companions, CEO Jeff Harmening said during a conference. "We believe the humanization of pets will continue leading pet parents to treat their pets more like members of the family," which will likely drive more spending in the premium pet food category, Harmening said. Pet food is a $52 billion business for General Mills in the U.S. and $130 billion globally. The company acquired White Bridge in December to expand its offerings of wet food for cats, which is the fastest growing pet food category in the U.S. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1134 ET - Southwest Airlines' decision to shed about 1,750 jobs is hard to square away like the others it's issued since its September investor day, says Melius Research's Conor Cunningham and Patrick Coleman in a research note. The analysts say the culture instilled at the carrier is a big factor in its successful history. "The news yesterday, to terminate 15% of corporate positions, goes against much of that culture the company has cultivated over its 53-year history," the analysts say. Other changes at Southwest included eliminating its open seating policy, which came in the wake of activist Elliott Investment Management's stake in the company. "A delicate balance is very much needed here, as culture is difficult to cultivate but easy to ruin," say the analysts. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1112 ET - H&E Equipment's fleet size and age is a good way for an acquirer to bring down its fleet costs. Steven Ramsey of Thompson Research Group tells WSJ that H&E has a sizable fleet spread across a large branch network, with a fleet age that is much younger than the industry and younger than that of United Rentals and Herc Holdings. "The capex needed to replace the fleet of H&E can be much lower in the next few years, resulting in tremendous free cash flow to the acquirer," Ramsey says. The free cash flow could then be used for debt repayment, allowing the acquirer to pay a higher price. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1106 ET - Oppenheimer thinks that this may not be the end of the line for United Rental's pursuit of H&E Equipment Services. H&E received an offer from Herc Holdings for nearly $105 per share, Schneeberger says, or about $5.3 billion. This tops United Rentals' recent bid of about $4.8 billion. "H&E appears to be an attractive addition for both United Rental and Herc. We view it likely United Rental continues its pursuit of H&E," he says. H&E Equipment surges 15% and Herc Holdings is off 10%. United Rentals is down 3.7%. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

data.title: California's Biggest Home Insurer Threatens to Leave State Unless Its Rates Rise — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Bill Alpert

State Farm General Insurance was already in weak financial shape when wildfires swept through Southern California last month. Now, it is threatening to leave the state if California regulators won't let it raise premiums.

That is a problem. As the state's biggest home insurer, with a 20% market share and more than a million homeowner customers, State Farm's departure would leave a big hole in California's insurance market.

"These fires will collectively be the costliest in the history of the company," said State Farm in its Feb. 3 request for a 22% hike in its homeowners' insurance rate "to avert a dire situation for our customers and the insurance market in the state of California."

The state's insurance department advised California insurance Commissioner Ricardo Lara to allow the rate hike on an interim basis, with refunds to homeowners if later hearings proved the rates unjustified.

On Friday, Lara demurred. State Farm stopped writing new California policies in 2023, he noted, and started canceling 30,000 policies last year. Yet the company's financial condition has still deteriorated.

"Other than rate increases, what other plans does State Farm have to address its financial challenges?" Lara asked, in his Feb. 14 response. Lara told the company to bring answers to that question to his office, on Feb. 26.

State Farm posted its unhappy reaction on its website. "While we're positioned to handle all of the claims associated with the most recent wildfires," it said, "State Farm General must seriously consider its options within the California insurance market going forward."

The State Farm rate showdown demonstrates that California is far from fixing its insurance crisis. For years, the rate increases allowed by the state's insurance department didn't keep pace with inflation and climate-related losses. Seven of the state's top dozen insurers stopped or restricted their sales of new policies. That pushed an increasing number of homeowners into the state's last-resort insurance pool, the Fair Access to Insurance Requirements Plan. The FAIR Plan just said it needs a $1 billion cash infusion to cover claims from last month's wildfires.

Insurance Commissioner Lara has tried to speed California's rate reviews. Since the start of the Los Angeles fires, the state approved homeowners' rate increases of 30% for the Allstate Corp., and 12% for Mercury General.

Financial problems plagued State Farm General for years before the recent wildfires. The company is a California affiliate of State Farm Mutual Automobile Insurance, the large mutual insurance company that's owned by its policyholders.

State Farm General says that its surplus capital for paying claims was $1 billion at the end of 2024. That is only half its 2022 level, and a quarter its 2016 level. In the nine years since 2016, the insurer has lost a cumulative $5.3 billion writing insurance, or $1.26 in claims and expenses for every $1 of premium it collected.

In June 2024, the insurance company had asked California for a 30% rate hike, under a state rule created for insurers whose solvency is threatened. It was still awaiting a decision when the January wildfires broke out. In its request for emergency action this month, State Farm said that it has already received more than 8,700 claims from the LA fires and has paid over $1 billion to customers.

"We are very disappointed the Commissioner ignored his department's recommendation to take the critical and necessary step to approve State Farm General's request for interim rate increases associated with our June 2024 filings," said the insurer.

Write to Bill Alpert at william.alpert@barrons.com

data.title: Huntsman CORP SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Huntsman Corporation, a global manufacturer and marketer of differentiated chemicals, has released its annual Form 10-K report, detailing the company's financial and operational performance for the fiscal year. The report provides insights into Huntsman's revenue streams, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in a competitive and volatile market environment.

Financial Highlights

Huntsman Corporation reported total revenues of $6,036 million, a 1% decrease compared to the previous year, primarily due to lower average selling prices across all segments. The company's gross profit stood at $866 million, reflecting a 4% decline from the previous year, mainly due to reduced gross profits in the Performance Products segment.

Operating income saw a significant downturn, with a loss of $25 million compared to an income of $84 million in the previous year, impacted by restructuring costs and a prepaid asset write-off. Net income also experienced a substantial decline, with a net loss of $127 million compared to a net income of $153 million in the previous year, influenced by discontinued operations and increased interest expenses.

Net income attributable to Huntsman Corporation was a loss of $189 million, compared to $101 million in the previous year, driven by losses from continuing operations. Basic and diluted net income per share attributable to Huntsman Corporation both stood at a loss of $1.10, compared to $0.57 in the previous year, reflecting the overall decline in net income.

Business Highlights

Huntsman operates in three main segments: Polyurethanes, Performance Products, and Advanced Materials. Each segment contributes to the company's diverse product offerings. The Polyurethanes segment focuses on MDI products, polyols, and TPU, while the Performance Products segment specializes in amines and maleic anhydride. The Advanced Materials segment offers epoxy, phenoxy, acrylic, and polyurethane formulations.

The company has a significant global presence with major manufacturing facilities in the U.S., Europe, and China. The Chinese market is particularly critical, being the largest MDI market in the world. Huntsman has a joint venture with Sinopec to support long-term demand growth in the region.

The Polyurethanes segment has seen volume growth driven by global economic activity and the substitution of MDI-based products for other materials. Huntsman produces over 2,400 distinct MDI-based polyurethane products. Additionally, the company completed the separation and acquisition of assets of the Shanghai Liengheng Isocyanate Company Ltd. joint venture, now operating an independent manufacturing facility in Caojing, China, producing crude MDI.

Looking ahead, Huntsman anticipates continued growth in MDI and formulated MDI systems driven by megatrends such as energy management and urbanization. The company remains focused on expanding differentiated product offerings and enhancing customer support and technical service through strategically located systems houses.

Strategic Initiatives

Huntsman Corporation has undertaken several strategic initiatives, including the separation and acquisition of assets from the SLIC joint venture, resulting in a net bargain purchase gain. The company also implemented cost realignment and synergy plans, particularly in Europe, to optimize its cost structure and achieve annualized cost savings exceeding $280 million. Additionally, Huntsman completed the sale of its Textile Effects Business to Archroma for $597 million.

In terms of capital management, Huntsman issued $350 million of 5.70% senior notes due 2034 to support general corporate purposes, including debt repayment. The company also repurchased $353 million of its common stock under its share repurchase program, which has $547 million remaining under the current authorization. Huntsman declared dividends totaling $1.00 per share for the year and maintained a strong liquidity position with $1,719 million in combined cash and unused borrowing capacity.

Looking ahead, Huntsman plans to spend between $180 million to $190 million on capital expenditures in 2025, focusing on environmental, health, and safety upgrades, as well as expansions and upgrades of existing facilities. The company also anticipates a liquidating distribution of approximately $40 million from the SLIC joint venture in 2025. Additionally, Huntsman is preparing for the maturity of its €300 million 2025 Senior Notes and expects to make contributions of approximately $35 million to its pension and postretirement benefit plans.

Challenges and Risks

Huntsman operates in a highly competitive industry with significant reliance on global economic conditions, particularly in the U.S., Europe, and Asia. The cyclicality and volatility of the markets for many of its products, such as those used in aerospace, housing, and construction, pose ongoing challenges. Additionally, the company faces risks related to the availability and cost of raw materials, which are influenced by geopolitical factors and market conditions.

The company is exposed to a variety of risks that could materially and adversely affect its business, including:

  • Economic Risks: Global economic instability, inflation, elevated interest rates, and recessions could significantly impact demand for the company's products.
  • Competitive Risks: The company faces intense competition from larger chemical companies with greater financial resources, which could affect its market position and profitability.
  • Operational Risks: Disruptions in production due to natural disasters, strikes, or supply chain issues could materially impact operations.
  • Regulatory Risks: Compliance with evolving environmental, health, and safety regulations, including those related to greenhouse gas emissions, could increase operational costs.
  • Cybersecurity Risks: The company relies on information technology systems that are vulnerable to cyberattacks, which could disrupt operations and lead to financial losses.
  • International Business Risks: Fluctuations in currency exchange rates and changes in foreign trade policies could adversely affect financial results.
  • Indebtedness Risks: Changes in credit ratings could increase borrowing costs and limit access to capital markets.

Management acknowledges the challenges posed by economic conditions and competitive pressures. Strategies to address these include focusing on innovation, enhancing customer relationships, and optimizing the global footprint to better utilize assets and systems houses. The company is also investing in technology security initiatives and disaster recovery plans to mitigate cybersecurity risks.

The company is exposed to market risks related to foreign currency exchange rate fluctuations, which may adversely affect its financial results. Additionally, significant price volatility or interruptions in the supply of raw materials and energy could increase costs that may not be fully passed on to customers, impacting profitability.

SEC Filing: Huntsman CORP [ HUN ] - 10-K - Feb. 18, 2025

data.title: Ackman Says He Will Unveil Deal to Create Modern Version of Berkshire Hathaway — Barrons.com
data.description:
Andrew Bary

Howard Hughes Holdings shares have popped in afternoon trading after investor Bill Ackman tweeted on X that he would be announcing a "potential transaction" at 4 p.m. Eastern time to create a modern version of Berkshire Hathaway.

The tweet is an apparent reference to his proposal, which has been considered by the Howard Hughes board, for Ackman to acquire control of the company through purchase of additional shares and merge it with a newly formed unit of Ackman's Pershing Square Holdco.

Howard Hughes shares are up 4% Monday to $78.60, and the move came after 3 p.m. Eastern time when the Ackman tweet was posted on X. Pershing Square now owns close to 40% of Howard Hughes.

Ackman has proposed to pay $85 a share for about $1 billion of stock. It is possible that Ackman may make a revised proposal.

"At 4pm, we are going to announce a potential transaction which, if completed, will provide me and my firm with the opportunity to create our own, you might say, modern-day version of Berkshire. Fortunately, our starting base of assets won't be a dying textile company, but a very good business," Ackman tweeted. "We will adopt similar, long-term, shareholder-oriented principles to Berkshire, and we intend to hold the stock forever."

Write to Andrew Bary at andrew.bary@barrons.com

data.title: Westfield Specialty International CUO England exiting after a little more than a year
data.description:
(The Insurer) - Joe England, CUO of Westfield Specialty International, is leaving the company after a year and a half.

Based in London, England joined Westfield Specialty in September 2023, reporting to Graham Evans, executive vice president and head of international insurance.

Previously, he was divisional director of marine, energy and specialty at Hiscox, where he spent just over a year. Before that, he served as CEO of Vibe Syndicate Management and group CEO of Syndicate Holding Corp.

England also spent more than a decade at Axis Capital, where he worked with a number of Westfield Specialty executives, including president Jack Kuhn, and where England ultimately served as CUO for Axis’ international division.

Earlier in his career, he spent 14 years at Chubb predecessor Ace Global Markets, where he was head of UK and Ireland marine and a member of the Ace UK management team.

Westfield Specialty’s international division was formed largely out of its February 2023 acquisition of Argo’s Lloyd’s Syndicate 1200 for $125 million, and which had around $650 million in premium at the time of acquisition.

The Insurer broke the news last week Westfield Specialty ended 2024 with gross written premium of $1.6 billion and a combined ratio of 95.1% across its US and international portfolio and hired an accident and health (A&H) team led by Chris DiSipio to spur its entry into the class.
